transcription:
When to the gift of genius is added the gift of the power to use it, what may we not expect from one so richly endowed? Mr. Oscar Wilde to a poetic imagination adds the brilliancy which not even the most prejudiced among the 'hated Saxons' can refuse to acknowledge as a product of Ireland. Then, to make the balance true, we have in him the addition of Oxford 'Culture,' whereby natural genius is reduced to working order; and, from a wilde jungle, transformed into an exquisite pleasure ground, not too prim and precise for fancy's bright children to wander in, or too mundane and commonplace for the gambols of the fairies at midnight. In The Happy Prince and other stories, four in number, which make up this charming volume Mr. Wilde shows that he has not only a poetic imagination and brilliant wit, but a remarkably keen eyesight into human nature. Those who have the pleasure of knowing Mr. Wilde will be in no way surprised to find the critical faculty in him sharp as a two-edged sword ; those who know him only as the great Apostle of the Æsthetes, posing perpetually with a “ Lily in his medieval hand ” will be perplexed and astonished to find in these fables evidences that the author is as well versed in the faults and foibles of men and women as he is in the mystery of beauty, and in the precious secrets she whispers to those of her worshippers who are ardent and true.
 For myself, I may as well confess honestly I never expected to read from the pen of Mr. Oscar Wilde such a story as ' The Selfish Giant.' That it is beautifully told in pure and simple English goes without saying, but it carries in the exquisite heart of it the true meaning of the good news of God revealed to us by the death on Calvary—the good news that Love is not only the vanquisher of Death, but the one means whereby we rise to the life immortal. How exquisite is the description of the ice-bound garden of the Giant. I must quote some of it :—" Then the spring came ...
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales

transcription:
All readers of the Philistine persuasion will do well to avoid "The House of Pomegranates." To read it will simply rub them up the wrong way and upset them for days. Indeed, I do not know to whom I ought to recommend it. It is over the heads of the majority of children, and yet it is not, I imagine, intended for the mature. The stories are full of fancy and poetic thought, but it is possible to read between the lines and to find meanings which, if put into the text, would have spoiled the allegories. Mr. Wilde is far too wise to allow his morals to be obtrusive.
The stories in the volume are four in number, and my favourites are "The Fisherman and His Soul" and "The Birthday of the Infanta." In the former are some masterly bits of description, and the moral of the tale (if the author will excuse me for mentioning that rococo word a second time)—namely, that it is far better to be under the guidance of love without a soul than to be soulful without love—is admirable.
Of the illustrations I frankly confess I am not sufficiently "advanced" to speak. No doubt they are beautiful, but my eyes are holden and I cannot see.
collection: A House of Pomegranates

transcription: the prettiest child's story-book we have had since Alice in Wonderland. Like its famous predecessor, it is as readable and as intelligible by 'grown-up' people as by those to whom it is supposed to be addressed, and is flavoured by its author, Mr. Oscar Wilde, with pleasantest wit and most graceful humour.
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales
publication_name: The World: A Journal for Men and Women

transcription:
Of all writers Mr. Wilde must be judged by his aim to be enjoyed. He is not a writer for the easily irritated, or the man who expects to order his novelists about like shopmen. People with strong preconceptions, and water-tight formulas may as well give him up at once. It is only people with bend in them, those who don't mind what he says so long as he says it piquantly, it is only those who "ever know the inmost shrine of Art"—as practised by Oscar Wilde.
It is such an obliging reader who would venture to tell him that, with every admission, these stories must never be placed on the same shelf with "Dorian Gray," the "Intentions," the charming "Fairy Tales," or those early "Poems." That they can contain wit goes without saying, but then a story should be more than a succession of sparks; that the central ideas of the first and second—"The Sphinx without a Secret"—are comic is admitted, and the latter is certainly the best of the four; but Mr. Wilde has not been patient enough in his treatment. He has attempted burlesque—and burlesque a form now practised at present by many painstaking people, who thus earn

A PRECARIOUS LIVELIHOOD.

Mr. Wilde would not take the bread out of their mouths! No; he is too real a humorist to be allowed this descent to burlesque. We, his admirers, must sorrowfully, but firmly, forbid it. Let him give us a comedy, if he will—some more "Intentions," or another story-criticism like "The Portrait of Mr. W. H."; but no more "Lord Arthur Savile's Crimes." The reader may be curious as to what Lord Arthur's crime was. Well, being out one evening at Lady Windermere's he must perforce submit to examination by that lady's new lion a cheiromantist, Mr. Podgers.
"Dear me, you are so original," had murmured the Duchess of Paisley, "trying to remember what a cheiromantist really was, and hoping it was not the same as a cheiropodist."
Mr. Podgers, on examining "the hand," was noticed by Lord Arthur to turn pale, but he managed to smooth it over and give him a commonplace reading. However, come Podgers by himself, and offering him a big cheque, he extorted the truth.

MURDER

was written upon his hand. Lord Arthur became appropriately desperate, and felt at once that with such a doom menacing him it would be wrong for him to prolong his engagement with the lovely Sybil Merton. The only alternative, since the deed apparently must be done, was to get it over as soon as possible. It is hard, but it is duty, it is "for her sake." He thereupon makes two unsuccessful attempts on a dowager aunt and his venerable uncle the Dean of Chichester; but the deed being yet to do, he roams in deep dejection one night along the Thames Embankment. And, behold, there is Mr. Podgers leaning dreamily over the parapet. A brilliant idea comes to Lord Arthur. Quickly seizing him by the calves, he topples him over into the stream. A splash, a gurgle, a floating hat, and all is over. The prophet has thus fulfilled his own prophecy. An evening paper confirms the suicide of the famous cheiromantist, and his fate thus fulfilled, Lord Arthur, released at last, flies to the arms of his dear Sybil to offer her his future—now without a cloud. The point of the story is, of course, in its sub-title—"A Study of Duty."
collection: Lord Arthur Savile’s Crime and Other Stories

transcription:
[The] Young King; the Birthday of the Infanta," [and] other Beautiful Tales," writes Mr. Wilde as [a cap]tion over his "House of Pomegranates." [A de]lightful touch of a charming vanity, which [refers] to itself at itself. Those who have [heard] Mr. Wilde lecture know the smile. [It is] the smile of a Narcissus with a gift of [talk.] Narcissus is sometimes born so, and then [is] saved from drowning, and lives to write [a charm]ing autobiography, or give us some other [gift] of his dear self with a daring [hand] that wins the world. So Mr. Wilde has [built] us this "house not made with [hands,"] "this house of pomegranates gathered in [hi]s garden. And with him went two dream-[mak]ing artists, by name Ricketts and Shannon, [who in] a nook of the green place they dreamed [in the] grass, and builded the house together [to the] sound of the double flute. Forgive [me if] this reads more like a fairy [tale] than a review. Were I to write to [him f]or at this moment, I must begin "Fair Sir," [and] truly I should bid him see that "seven [stars] were woven into my robe." [What] a marvellous eye Mr. Wilde has for such [minute] detail, and what a knowledge of precious [things!] His memory must surely be like the treasure [chambe]rs of his own King of Ashter, wherein were— [the] tortoiseshells full of pearls, and hollowed [ivory] ones of great size piled up with red rubies. [Gold] was stored in coffers of elephant hide, and [fragrant] dust in leather bottles. There were opals [and sap]phires, the former in cups of crystal, and [the latt]er in cups of jade. Round green emeralds [were arrang]ed in order upon thin plates of ivory, and in [a corner] were silk bags filled, some with turquoise [gems] and others with beryls. . . . And yet [you] have [here] but a tithe of what was there." [He] has often admired the dainty magic with [which] Keats was able to transmute mere confec-[tion] into— Candied apple, quince, and plum, and gourd;
Jellies smoother than the creamy curd,
[And] lucent syrops, tinct with cinnamon." [He] possessed the same power, especially over [richly] vowelled names. "These delicates" are [handle]d "with glowing hand" all through these [beauti]ful stories. The hand is perhaps too glowing [in its] profusion. It is apt to neglect proportion. [Nev]er mind that, read this sentence from "The [Birth]day of the Infanta." Maybe you have known [it l]ong ago—that sentence telling of the wood-[land] life of the little dwarf:— [When t]he wind dances he knew—the mad dance [of enter]tainment with the autumn, the light dance in [the s]andals over the corn, the dance with white [snow] wreaths in winter, and the blossom dance [throug]h the orchards in spring. [The] tragedy of a toadstool, that dreamed it was [a] rose worthy to be worn in the little [Infanta']-s bosom—is the story of the poor [little] dwarf, who never having seen his face, and knowing nought of a cul-[ture]d passion for the ugly, dreamed that the [Infanta] loved him for his beauty, when she laughed [to wat]ch him dance. Then, seeking her through [the] place, he comes to a mirrored room, where all [things] strangely repeat themselves. "Was it Echo? [Did] she mock the eye, as she mocked the voice?" [So] tenderly does Mr. Wilde tell how at last he [finds] himself in the monstrous little reflection [that mi]mics him in the glass, very tenderly does he [lay] his little broken-hearted figure upon the floor. [But] why need we choose no favourite out of these four [tales]? — but this and "The Fisherman and his Soul" [are fi]ne. The latter, the longest and most elab-[orate]ly-wrought of the four, and "The Star-[Child"] — I have not, I believe, previously appeared. ["The] Fisherman and his Soul" strikes one first [as one] of good fairy tales, with its simplicity of [manner], and then, how strange to watch the various [on]es shooting out from the one smooth stem, [hung] with clusters of blossom and strange [birds] and songs. It is art's miracle of the mango— [I] speak of the "get-up" and "illustrations" [of this] book as things apart seems absurd, for, [indeed,] this is one of those rare books of which we [speak of] not the body from the soul. It is a unity. [It] should really be spoken of as a whole. [The] cover, designs, and stories seem not separate [things,] but only different ways of expressing the [same] strange fantasy. But I have spoken of the [stories] apart. Why not the pictures? But they [are hard]er for me to write about than the stories. [Especial]ly Mr. Shannon's dim "dreams" or rather [the sil]very ghosts of dreams. There is something [very] fascinating about these silvery grey [impres]sions, faint as the faintest grey of dawn—so [faint] that one at first almost takes the page for a [blank.] But gradually they grow on one like [the] fields in the moonlight. And the best [of] them is actually of the moonlight, [where] the fisherman far away out on the moonlit [sea dr]aws up the little mermaid in his net and [wears] such a secret sweet smile, as he doats on [his st]range haul. Mr. Ricketts' quaint symbolic [art no] less beautifully accompanies that more [narrat]ive side of Mr. Wilde's writing of which I [have sp]oken. Are we never to have another num-[ber of] "The Germ"?
collection: A House of Pomegranates

transcription: The humour of Oscar Wilde's jeu d'esprit thus entitled is of a very different order. As pure farce---and what can be better than farce at its best?---it deserves to live; for it is independent of passing circumstances, and is written with all the grave, simple, matter of fact seriousness which is more essential to farce than to tragedy. Nobody with the slightest sense of humour, or, for that matter, nobody with the strongest, can fail to enjoy the story of a man to whom murder presented itself in the light of a simple duty. It is worth all Mr. Wilde's serious work put together. The stories which follow are also excellent, and their author is to be congratulated on having introduced an entirely new and original ghost to the world---no slight feat in these days. But for its degeneration into sentiment, the story in which it appears would be almost as good in its way as that of 'Lord Arthur's Crime'.
collection: Lord Arthur Savile’s Crime and Other Stories
publication_name: The Graphic: An Illustrated Weekly Newspaper

transcription:
In the volume before us Mr. Oscar Wilde comes forward in a new and favourable light as a writer of fairy tales. 'The 'Happy Prince' and the four accompanying tales are full of graceful fancies and quaint conceits, and will afford equal pleasure to grown-up and to youthful readers. High spirits and buoyancy are not to be looked for in these stories, but the best of them are marked by pathos and poetic feeling, relieved by humorous touches, generally of a very happy kind. The two concluding tales are written in a satirical strain that borders on the cynical; but the selfishness and folly at which they are aimed are fair objects for satire.
 The statue of the Happy Prince stood high above the city on a tall column. ' He was gilded all over with thin leaves of fine gold, for eyes he bad two bright sapphires, and a large ruby glowed on his sword-hilt'. A swallow, off to Egypt for the winter, alighted on the statue. His rest was disturbed by the tears that fell from the eyes of the Happy Prince, weeping over a poor mother and her sick child. 'Swallow', said the Prince, who must have been a countryman of Mr. Wilde's, 'will you not bring (sic) her the ruby out of my sword-hilt?' The swallow did not like it at first, but she consented to stay and bring---or take---the ruby to the poor widow. Next day the swallow was anxious to be off, but the Prince pressed him to stay and do another kindness.
 [The Poor Student]
 ...
 The Selfish Giant
 ' The Selfish Giant' is a very happy and beautiful apologue, which is not without a present application.
 ...
 The giant got no Spring or Summer until he let the children in, It would be well if a like treatment could be applied to some giants with empty gardens not a hundred miles from Fleet-street. But the prettiest story among the number is that of 'The Nightingale and the Rose'. A young student was searching everywhere for a red rose, and could not find one. The girl he loved bad promised to dance with him if he brought her one. What, he thought, was the use of all his learning if for the want of a red rose his life was made wretched. 'Here, at last, is a true lover', said the Nightingale. 'Night after night have I sang of him, though I knew him not; night after night have I told his story to the stars, and now I see him'. And she resolved to find him a rose, even though (as it turned out) it was at the sacrifice of her life.
 ...
 The Student and the Nightingale
 But the young lady was hardly worthy of such a sacrifice. 'Iam afraid it will not go with my dress', she answered; 'and, besides, the Chamberlain's nephew has sent me some real jewels, and everybody knows that jewels cost far more than flowers'. So the poor student threw the rose into the gutter, and was fain to act on the advice proffered to Rousseau, to eave ladies alone and study mathematics.
 We must leave the reader to make further acquaintance for himself with the pleasant conceits to be found in Mr. Wilde's book; with theduck who instructed her young : ' You will never be in the best society unless you can stand on your heads'; with the remarkable rocket who knew he would make a sensation; and with the swallow of which all the Sparrows said : 'What a distinguished stranger' so that he enjoyed himself very much. The volume is very tastefully bound and printed, and is illustrated by such masters of the craft as Mr. Walter Crane and Mr, Jacomb Hood. Both its own merits and the well-known Personality of the author are likely, we imagine, to secure for [sic] it a deserved success among the lighter literature of the season.
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales
publication_name: The Literary World: Choice Readings from the Best New Books, with Critical Reviews

transcription: The gift of writing fairy tales is rare, and Mr. Oscar Wilde shows that he possesses it in a rare degree. The Happy Prince, and other Stories, are full of charming fancies and quaint humour. Though with a distinct character of their own, they are not unworthy to compare with Hans Andersen, and it is not easy to give higher praise than this. There is a piquant touch of contemporary satire which differentiates Mr. Wilde from the teller of pure fairy tales; but it is so delicately introduced that the illusion is not destroyed and a child would delight in the tales without being worried or troubled by their application, while children of larger growth will enjoy them and profit by them. The illustrations are charming.
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales

transcription: There is a glut of description and epithet in Mr. Oscar Wilde's book of fairy tales which leaves the reader with a strong sense of having fed on lucent syrup tinct with cinnamon, and baring bad too much of it. No language can compare with it except Ouida's when she is giving an inventory of the properties environing one of her heroines. Few basal ideas in fairy stories can bear this treatment; certainly not Mr. Wilde's. To make matters worse, ho has chosen to relate them in 4 style paraphrasing Scripture, which gives to the whole the air of complexity aping simplicity without success. The weird decorations sown with studied carelessness among the margins heighten this effect, as do also the medieval illustrations and the Grosvenor-Gallery covers Mr. Wilde seems, on the whole, to have taken bis stories something over-solemnly. In a less ostentatious se ting [sic], certain really poetic ideas found floating about in the volume would have gone further towards redeeming the inconsequent and spasmodic construction. The descriptions, in the opening story, of the weaving of the King's robe 'on the loom of Sorrow and by the white hands of Pain', 'the blood in the heart of the Raby, and Death in the heart of be Pearl', have shuddering force, and are among the few things in the book that leave an afterglow in the mind.
collection: A House of Pomegranates

transcription:
We fear that Mr. Oscar Wilde's enemies (supposing him to be capable of possessing any) must have seen in this handsome book a deliberate provocation to the bourgeois au front glabre. Amid the pomegranates and other trimmings on the cover, there is a back view of a peacock which looks for all the world like that of an elderly spinster with her hands behind her back, and her profile turned towards the beholder. Mr. Shannon and Mr. Ricketts have sprinkled the pages with devices rare and strange in the latest and straitest school of Neo-Preraphaelitism, and the chief illustrations in the book are of a most absolute fancy. Mr. Wilde has, we observe, protested in the public press against the judgment that they are invisible, and, strictly speaking, they are not. But being printed in very faint grisaille on very deeply cream-tinted plate paper, they put on about as much invisibility as is possible to things visible, and as they are arranged, neither facing letterpress nor with the usual tissue guard, but with a blank sheet of paper of the same tint and substance opposite them, a hasty person might really open the leaves and wonder which side the illustration was. Nevertheless, we rather like them, for when you can see them, they are by no means uncomely, and they suit their text---a compliment which we are frequently unable to pay to much more commonplace instances of the art of book illustration. 
 In the case of the text, also, hasty judgment is likely to be unduly harsh judgment. The pomegranates that compose the house---the grains that make up the pomegranate would have been a better metaphor---are four in number, and are all tales of the Märchen order, though one is something even more of a fabliau than of a Märchen. This is called 'The Birthday of the Infanta', and tells, to put it very shortly, how a certain little Spanish princess had an ugly dwarf who loved her, and died of a broken heart when he found out, not only how ugly he himself was, but how his beloved mistress thought of him as nothing but a fantastic toy. 'Tis an ower true tale. But we are not sure that Mr. Wilde's manner of telling it is quite the right one. The first and the last of the four, 'The Young King', and 'The Star Child', are pretty enough moralities; the first of halfmediaeval, half-modern Socialist strain. The other tells how a child was cured of cruelty, partly by some metaphysical aid, partly (we do not know whether Mr. Wilde intended to draw this part of the moral, but he has) by sound beatings and a not excessive allowance of bread and water. 
 
 But the third piece, 'The Fisherman and his Soul', is much longer, as long, indeed, as any two of them, and to our fancy a good deal better. It tells how a fisherman fell in love with a mermaid, and, to gain her, consented to part with, but not in the ordinary fashion to sell, his soul; how after a time he grew weary of his happiness, went to look after his soul, and found her, divorced as she was from his or any heart, a rather unpleasant, not to say immoral, companion; how he in vain endeavoured to return once more to his mermaid and only found her dead, when he and she and the soul were reunited once for all; and how, when the dead bodies of the pair were found and buried in unhallowed ground, there came a miracle converting to charity the heart of the parish priest who had cast them out. The separate ingredients of the piece are, of course, not very novel; but, to tell the truth, the separate ingredients of a story of this kind hardly can be, and Mr. Wilde has put them together with considerable skill, and communicated to the whole an agreeable character. The little mermaid is very nice, both when she is caught literally napping, and when she sings, and when she explains the necessity of her lover parting with his soul if he will have her. Also the young witch (to whom, when the parish priest has, not unnaturally, declined to unsoul him, the fisherman goes) is pleasing. She had red hair, and in gold tissue embroidered with peacock's eyes and a little green velvet cap she must have looked very well. The Sabbath, too, is good (there are too few Sabbaths in English), though the gentlemanly Satan is not new. Good, too, is the business-like manner in which the fisherman separates his soul from him by a device not impossibly suggested by one Adelbert von Chamisso, a person of ability. The adventures of the discarded and heartless soul are of merit, and it is a very good touch to make the fisherman's final, and hardly conscious, desertion of his mermaid-love turn on nothing more than a sudden fancy to dance, and the remembrance that she had no feet and could not dance with him. It is particularly satisfactory to learn that the mermaid's tail was of pearl-and-silver. There has been an impression in many circles that mermaids' tails are green, and we have always thought that it would be unpleasant to embrace a person with a green tail. But pearl-and-silver is quite different.
collection: A House of Pomegranates

transcription: We do not like the outside of the cover of Mr Oscar Wilde's House of Pomegranates (Osgood) [sic]. The Indian club with a house-painter's brush on the top which passes muster for a peacock, and the chimney-pot hat with a sponge in it, which is meant to represent a basket containing a pomegranate, or a fountain, or something of that kind, are grotesque, but not ideally so. The inside of the cover, however, with its olive sheaves of corn falling apart, its fluttering quails, and crawling snails, delights the eye. So do the pictures and the type and the paper. Mr Ricketts has learned the art of drawing dreams and visions, and Mr Shannon can make decorative designs full of charming detail. We can well believe that the book is as delightful as it looks.
collection: A House of Pomegranates
publication_name: The Speaker: A Review of Politics, Letters, Science and the Arts

transcription: The first of Mr. Oscar Wilde's allegories or parables, or whatever he may call them, is admirable. A young king, who is a passionate lover of the beautiful, dreams on the eve of his coronation sundry dreams in which he sees how the splendors on which his heart is set are won by the sufferings and death of thousands of unknown toilers. So struck is he by these visions, that he will have none of the glories with which it is intended to adorn him for his coronation. He goes to the church with a rude cloak of sheepskin and a leathern tunic, and a wreath of wild briar round his head; but the sunlight streaming through the painted window envelops him with such a splendor that none can refuse to honor him. The 'Birthday of the Infanta' shows the pathos of a loving soul lodged in a deformed body. As to 'The Fisherman and his Soul', we cannot exactly see the scope of it. The fisherman gets rid of his soul in order to win the love of a mermaid, and the soul sent out without a heart commits all kinds of atrocities. That is a fine idea; but the purport of the whole eludes us. Mr. Wilde writes, as usual, in a highly ornate style, often beautiful, but somewhat fatiguing.
collection: A House of Pomegranates

transcription: This is a clever book by a clever man, who has not yet altogether done justice to himself. Mr. Wilde can scarcely have intended these five tales, which (notably the first) are illustrated by Mr. Walter Crane and Mr. Jacomb Hood in a style which may be described as impressionism tempered by elegance, for boys and girls. Their note is melancholy, and a subtle sarcasm pervades them which will puzzle young readers. Take, as a specimen of this sarcasm, these two sentences from the first page of the first and best of the stories, "The Happy Prince :’— He [the statue of the Prince] was very much admired indeed. ‘He is as beautiful as a weathercock,’ remarked one of the Town Councillors, who wished to gain a reputation for having artistic tastes; ‘only not quite so useful,’ he added, fearing lest people should think him unpractical, which he really was not.” Mr. Wilde’s sarcasm occasionally seems akin to cynicism, as in " The Nightingale and the Rose,” which is a story of self-sacrifice wasted. The teaching conveyed in most of the tales, particularly in "The Happy Prince,” "The Selfish Giant,” and "The Re- markable Rocket,” is essentially sound, being to the effect that unselfishness is moral beauty, and that vain display is moral ugliness. In the literature of fable, there has probably appeared nothing so pathetic as the story of the little swallow which is included in that of "The Happy Prince.” The public will look forward with much interest to Mr. Oscar Wilde’s giving them a second instalment [sic] of his dainty wisdom.
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales

transcription:
...
 Thackeray and Kingsley both tried their hands at what, for the sake of a better and more comprehensive word, we call fairy-tales. The ' Rose and the Ring', and the 'Waterbabies', have become 'classics of the nursery ' and schoolroom, and for many years there have been floods of translations from Russian, Scandinavian, and Eastern sources, to say nothing of originals like 'Alice in Wonderland', or Lord Brabourne's and Mr. Andrew Lang's *märchen*. Mrs. Molesworth's Four-Winds Farm is a delightful book, and Mr. Oscar Wilde has written some very imaginative stories, perhaps more ethical in their suggestive meaning than we might have expected from a cynical dramatist. His House of Pomegranates is in some ways akin to Dr. George Macdonald's collection, but is more mystical and less purely fanciful. It is sumptuously decorated and illustrated by Mr. C. Ricketts and Mr. Charles Shannon, and in the descriptive passages by which the author seeks to emulate in words the patient skill of the medieval illuminator, we recognise the hand of a leader of the modern esthetic school. The following from 'The Young King' is a good specimen of Mr. Wilde's decorative word-painting:
 
 >'The walls were hung with rich tapestries, representing the Triumph of Beauty. A large press, inlaid with agate and Lapislazuli, filled one corner, and facing the window stood a curiously wrought cabinet with lacquer panels of powdered and mosaiced gold, on which were placed some delicate goblets of Venetian glass anda cup of dark-veined onyx. Pale poppies were broidered on the silk coverlet of the bed, as though they had fallen from the tired hands of sleep, and tall reeds of fluted ivory bare up the velvet canopy, from which great tufts of ostrich plumes sprang, like white foam, to the pallid silver of the fretted ceiling. A laughing Narcissus, in green bronze, held a polished mirror above its head. On the table stood a flat bowl of amethyst'. 
 
 Such passages after a while pall on the reader, as a banquet chiefly composed of luscious fruits and perfumed wines would pall on the appetite, and cause longings for a cool draught of water and the simple fare of childhood. The 'Birthday of the Infanta' is the best of the four stories in Mr. Wilde's volume; it has a less artificial air than the others, and the contrast between the stately Spanish children surrounded by their courtly retinue, and the dwarf who for the first time comes face to face with his own ugly misshapen image, and breaks his heart at the discovery, is both pathetic and dramatic.
 But after all, it is to the old ' nursery classics' that we return with renewed zest, and best among all collections of old-world lore is Grimm's Household Tales. It is a veritable storehouse of legend and fairy-tale, we find most of our friends there, Cinderella perhaps a little different from her English namesake, but, at least, not barbarously maltreated as by the late George Cruickshank, who turned her story into a temperance tract. It seems necessary to scientific minds to discover scientific reasons for everything that exists, even for fairy-tales, but it is enough for most people to realise that they are the heritage of children, who, as a rule, prefer the old ' wild tales' to the most elaborate preparation of modern invention.
collection: A House of Pomegranates

transcription: Very pretty are the fables that Mr. Oscar Wilde has here strung together, tender and graceful, and not without a touch of humor, as in Mother Duck who 'kept saying to herlittle ones, 'You will never be in society unless you can stand on your heads,' and every now and then she showed them how it was done. But the little ducks paid no attention. They were so young that they did not know what an advantage it is to be in society."
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales

transcription: The Happy Prince and other tales, by Oscar Wilde, is a reproduction of this well-remembered person's fairy tales, in which there is really origiinality [sic] of imagination and cleverness in composition. The book is a thin one, the tales not being many in number. It is handsomely illustrated by Walter Crane and is beautifully printed.
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales

transcription: Mr Oscar Wilde's joke does not greatly amuse one. This little book has not the picturesque quality of 'The Picture of Dorian Gray,' which at times was, perhaps, garish. Nor is the humour of the dry and keen sort which flashes like rapiers—property rapiers, perhaps in the essays such as 'The Art of Lying.' There is plenty of humour, indeed, in 'The Canterville Ghost,' and how the American leasees of a baronial mansion dispossessed the ghost of Sir Simon de Canterville, who had murdered his wife in the 13th century, harassing him into giving up the ghost actually by rubbing out th eblood stains from the floor with Pinkerton's Champion Stain Remover, and offering him Rising Sun Lubricator to ease his chains from clanking, and so on. However, Mr Wilde's humour is of the exotic kind, and it is a long time ago since it ceased to be natural. Clever he must always be, but such a book as 'Lord Arthur Savile's Crime'will neither lay hold on the imagination or ticle any but the most passing sense of humour.
collection: Lord Arthur Savile’s Crime and Other Stories

transcription: The New York Mail and Express says in regard to 'The Happy Prince and Other Tales,' by Oscar Wilde: 'It was the fashion to laugh at Mr. Wilde when he was in this country, and no doubt that there was much that was laughable about him, but he was not the fool so many thought him, for he accomplished the object which brought him here—he made money. His verse was not free from the affectations of the period, but there were genuine touches in it, and one delightful lyric which his readers will recall. His forte, however, was prose, and just such prose as he gives us in this collection of fairy stories. There are five of them, 'The Happy Prince,' 'The Nightingale and the Rose', 'The Selfish Giant', 'The Devoted Friend', and 'The Remarkable Rocket.' They are prettily and brightly written, and ought to please the little lovers of folk-lore. The illustrations by Mr. Walter Crane and Mr. Jacomb Hood are charming.
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales

transcription:
Mr. Oscar Wilde, after having given the public some remarkable fiction and criticism, has returned to his old love, the poetic fairy tale, and in “A House of Pomegranates” exhibits a literary skill far in advance of that which went to the making of “The Happy Prince,” good as that work unquestionably was. “The Young King, The Birthday of the Infanta, and other Beautiful Tales” is the sub-title which Mr. Wilde, with a pardonable touch of vanity, gives to his book, which has evidently taxed all the cunning of the printer; for both in the matter of arrangement of type and illustrations it is daringly original. The larger drawings, by Mr. Shannon, depicting the subjects of each of the four tales, are as delicately fantastic as the tales themselves, and seem on a second study to grow in the imagination just as the tales themselves do. Of these the best in our opinion is the one which bears the title of “The Fisherman and his Soul,” though it is somewhat difficult to decide between this and “The Birthday of the Infanta,” which is highly poetic in its treatment, and does not appeal to the fantasy so much as the former tale. The plot of it is so slender that it would be unfair to reveal even its main outlines, but not the least pathetic portion is concerned with with a little dwarf who dances before  a princess on her birthday, and excites her to laughter, whereupon he, who has never seen his own face, imagines that the princess is in love with him.

Among other pretty things, his creator says of him [...]. Here is prose-poetry deftly written, and an art that knows how to conceal art. Sometimes, however, the straining after effect is obvious, as in the following description of the mermaid, who is the central figure in the delightful story of ‘The Fisherman and His Soul,' ... These caprices are but occasional, and they are not unpleasing to the adult reader who is not so foolish as to despise fairy tales, or to avoid the reading of good English prose when he can get it. Among the gift-books of the present season, 'The [sic] House of Pomegranates' deserves a high rank.
collection: A House of Pomegranates

transcription: Four tales in antique and with curious embellishments. Indeed, everything is pleasing about the volume except the plates, which are dim and unfinished-looking.
collection: A House of Pomegranates

transcription: Oscar Wilde has done some capital work in the 'Happy Prince and Other Tales'—a collection of five fairy stories, all told in simple style, but full of pathos and breathing the spirit of charity and good will. 'The Happy Prince', which gives the title to the book, is an admirable tale of the way a swallow and a statue helped to feed the hungry and clothe the destitute in a great city. The volume is artisically illustrated by Walter Crane and Jacob Hood.
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales

transcription: Oscar Wilde's little book 'Happy Prince and Other Tales,' is a very attractive piece of book-making. 'The Remarkable Rocket,' a delicate satire, is especially charming and is cleverly carried to a finish.
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales

transcription: The Happy Prince and Other Tales' is the title of a little volume by Oscar Wilde. The illustrations are by Walter Crane and Jacomb Hood. The contents include The Happy Prince, The Nightingale and the Rose, The Selfish Giant, The devoted [sic] Friend, and The Remarkable Rocket. The tales are apparently written for a purpose, and are more like elaborate fables than fairy stories in the ordinary meaning.
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales

transcription: We unblushingly confess that we were strongly prejudiced against this sumptuously-printed book by four full-page illustrations, for which, we believe, Mr. Ch. Shannon is responsible. If a tolerably thick tissue paper be placed over an ordinary woodcut the general effect of them will be fairly realised ; but when, by painful investigation, we get some glimmering of what the blurred and indistinct things aim at we search in vain for a single line or form that is beautiful, and for any meaning that is not idiotic. More preposterously perverse stuff was never printed and miscalled 'design' and 'decoration.' Some smaller illustrations, which we take to be by C. Ricketts (we sincerely trust we are not attributing these productions wrongly to the two coworkers), though not always quite right ore beautiful, always command our respect, and generally our admiration. They are somewhat too reminiscent of Dante Gabriel Rossetti, but they are most careful and painstaking, and the artist has always had a beautiful ideal in his mind. From the full-page rubbish no good can by any possibility come : from the artist who designed these small woodcuts very great things may reasonably be hoped. Over and over again we took up the book, but only to put it down again as those 'designs' sickened us. But we have mustered courage at last to try the text, and we find the stories really beautiful. But for whom are they written ? They take the guise of fairy stories—they deal with an impossible world, where dead thorns blossom, and men cut away their souls and live with mermaiden in the deep blue sea, and souls go wandering by themselves picking up wonderful lore, and all the laws of gravity and time and space and matter are nought—a world on which grown people sometimes cast a backward glance, half-pitying, half-envying their childhood selves which could believe all the fairness real. But the meanings infused into these fairy stories go deep, and touch matters of most grave and serious import. 'The Young King,' for instance, touches the problems of labour, the tyranny of wealth, of ague, and fever, and plague, and such grimly earnest and real things. We admire most the very pathetic story of the Infanta's Birthday—sad in its ending, but in workmanship daintily and delicately fine. All through the book shows real literary craftmanship of a high order.
collection: A House of Pomegranates

transcription: Oscar Wilde's first new book is to be a collection of short fairy tales for the holidays. It will be entitled 'The [sic] House of Pomegranates,' and is to be lavishly illustrated and brought out between gorgeous covers.
collection: A House of Pomegranates

transcription: Oscar Wilde has written some clever and readable fairy stories which are published under the title of 'The Happy Prince and other Tales' (Roberts, $1.00). The tales are, apparently, written with a purpose, and are more like elaborate fables than fiary stories in the ordinary meaning. The illustrations are by Walter Crane and Jacomb Hood.

transcription: Mr. Oscar Wylde [sic], though no longer the professed exponent of the principles of æstheticism, still keeps himself well before the public. Mr. Wylde has just published a short story [sic] entitled "Lord Arthur Savile's Crime." Of this book the critics say the writer takes neither himself, nor his tale, nor his readers seriously. "Lord Arthur Savile" is a "Bab Ballad" in prose. Two or three other stories are bound up in the same cover and none are above the average. While "'prentice hans" are advised to try short stories in the way of beginning, it is small encouragement to learn that not one short story in a hundred makes a mark. I met Mr. Oscar Wylde and his wife at a matinée last week—he still adipose and clean shaven, with an unctuous smile; she still pretty, and evidently appreciative of the distinction her husband's fame bestows upon her.
collection: Lord Arthur Savile’s Crime and Other Stories

transcription: The straiter sect of Oscarians may perhaps shake their heads over Mr. Oscar Wilde's book, [of] reprint, *Lord Arthur Savile's Crime, and Other Stories* (London: Osgood, McIlvaine, and Co ...). Except in the description of Lord Arthur's bathroom and bath, the usual wildings of the author's fancy are for the most part absent; there are few derangements of epitaphs, and the whole, even when it is paradoxical, is paradoxical in a simple and straightforward kind of way. The topsy-turvifying in the first story, if insisted on a little too long---the great danger in all these things,---is not unfunny. Lord Arthur Savile, a young man of great beauty (and of more personal goodness than sometimes appertains to Mr, Wilde's young men of great beauty), is engaged (also a good sign) to a girl of marvellous loveliness, Suddenly he is told by a cheiromancer that he will commit a murder. Lord Arthur's high-souled chivalry concludes that it would be base to marry with this doom banging over him, and that the only thing to do is to put the marriage off and get the murder over. 'The unexpected difficulties which arise and the final success may be leftto the reader. The thing, of course, is not original: De Quincey, Champfleury, Mr. Stevenson, and others may claiin royalty on it; but it is fairly done. The other chief story, 'The Canterville Ghost', is a rather quaint combination of a burlesque and a bluette---of Mark Twain and Musaeus, The poor ghost passes into the possession of an American family, and finds all his wonted terrors frustrated by their unfeeling modernity. Yet he is 'laid' at last in the most authentic and romantic manner by Virginia, the daughter of the house. The other two stories, 'A Sphinx Without a Secret' and 'The Model Millionaire', are much slighter, but by no means bad of their kind, As an example of what Mr. Wilde's writing is like when mask and domino are off, the book is interesting.
collection: Lord Arthur Savile’s Crime and Other Stories

transcription: This is a child's book of charming stories, aptly illustrated by Walter Crane and Jocomb [sic] Hood. There are five stories; "The Happy Prince," "The Nightingale and the Rose," "The Selfish Giant," "The Deserted [sic] Friend,"and "The Remarkable Rocket." The stories are all thoughtful stories, teaching good lessons, and worthy of the best words of praise for their literary excellence.
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales

transcription: Oscar Wilde has not lost a bit of the style which recently brought him so prominently before the public. In his book, "The Happy Prince and Other Tales," the reader will still discover the harmelss ideals of the knight of the sunflower. Dodd, Mead & Co., publishers. For sale in this city by Judd the bookseller
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales

transcription: Mr. Oscar Wilde has had so much abuse for certain calculated eccentricities in which he indulged during a tour of the United States that it is pleasant to find him on the title page of a book which can be praised without reserve. 'The Remarkable Rocket', 'The Devoted Friend,''The Selfish Giant', and 'The Nightingale and the Rose' are very dainty little fairy stories which will charm the young, yet afford zest to other persons by reason of the satirical, though not unkindly, flavor in them. 'The Happy Prince' has an element of pathos, such as we find in Hans Christian Andersen's unrivaled [sic] tales for children. The woodcuts are pleasant fancies, though somewhat used in the print, to employ a term that is often inexact when applied to pictures in modern books.
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales

transcription: Oscar Wilde, in constructing his 'House of Pomegranates,' uses as materials 'The Young King,' 'The Birthday of the Infanta,' 'The Fisherman and His Soul,' and 'The Star Child.' The first story relates to the young Italian Prince who, lapped in luxury, and with an intense love of the beautiful, sees at last how much sowrrow and misery are [sic] in this world. Steepted in pleasures, he at once renounces and, clad in a goatskin, wanders through the country. 'He is a dreamer of dreams,' cry his astonished people while mocking him. But his soul is chastened, and one day in a chapel, from a stained glass window, the light takes the shape of an angelic figure that crowns him, and then indeed is he King. The most charming of the stories is 'The Birthday of the Infanta.' Mr. Wilde is in sympathy with the sombre magnificence of old Spain. It is the Infanta's natal day, and before her dances the ugliest of dwarfs. He is not conscious how horrible is his guise. She laughs at his hideousness as would any child. She gives him a fower and he loses his heart. He wanders through the palace and sees for the first time his reflection in a mirror. He knows he is a misshapen and hunchbacked monster. 'Then he crawled, like some wounded thing, into the shadow, and lay there moaning.' The Infanta would have him dance again, and the Chamberlain smites him with his glove. The dwarf is motionless. 'Mi bella Princesa, your funny little dwarf will never dance again.' 'But why will he not dance again? ' asked the Infanta, laughing. 'Because his heart is broken,' answered the Chamberlain. 'For the future let those who come to play with me have no hearts,' she cried, and she ran into the garden. The make-up of the book is exceptionally good.
collection: A House of Pomegranates

transcription: Sir,---I have just been sent from London a copy of the *Pall Mall Gazette* containing a review of my book, '*A House of Pomegranates*'. The writer of this review makes a certain suggestion about my book which i beg you will allow me to correct at once. He starts by asking an extremely silly question, and that is whether or not I have written this book for the purpose of giving pleasure to the British child. Having expressed grave doubts on this subject, a subject on which I cannot conceive any fairly-educated person having any doubts at all, he proceeds, apparently quite seriously, to make the extremely limited vocabulary at the disposal of the British child the standard by which the prose of an artist is to be judged! Now in building this House of Pomegranates I had about as much intention of pleasing the British child as I had of pleasing the British public. Mamilius is as entirely delightful as Caliban is entirely detestable, but neither the standard of Mamilius nor the standard of Caliban is my standard. No artist recognizes any standard of beauty but that which is suggested by his own temperament. The artist seeks to realize in a certain material his immaterial idea of beauty, and thus to transform: an idea into an ideal. That is the way an artist makes things. That is why an artist makes things. The artist has no other object in making things. Does your reviewer imagine that Mr. Shannon, for instance, whose delicate and lovely illustrations he confesses himself quite unable to see, draws for the purpose of giving information to the blind ?---I remain, Sir, your obedient servant, Boulevard des Capucines, Paris. Oscar Wilde.
collection: A House of Pomegranates

transcription: Oscar Wilde's volume of poems was scarcely as successful as his book of fanciful tales, *A House of Pomegranates*, wherein the bewildering, gorgeous word painting bears no relation to the ideas or stories.
collection: A House of Pomegranates

transcription: Mr. Oscar Wilde appears to much better advantage as a writer of short tales, as seen in the new volume 'The Happy Prince and Other Tales,' than in—well, for instance, than in [sic] walking down Picadilly with a lily in his mediæval hand. The story which lends its name to the title rôle is full of delicate, beautiful fancy, and a vein of shrewd wit runs through it, while one entitled 'The Devoted Friend' is one of great pathos and power, and 'The Remarkable Rocket' is as piquant a little sarcasm as one can imagine, brilliantly embodied.
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales

transcription: The Happy Prince and Other Tales is the name of a story book by Oscar Wilde. It has five stories in it, illustrated by Walter Crane and Jacomb Hood. Oscar's tales are not too bad, and will remind the reader of Hans Andersen.
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales

transcription: Five excellent fairy tales are grouped together in this volume, with illustrations by Walter Crane and Jacomb Hood. The stories are happy conceits worked out in a way sure to captivate imaginative children and give them much pleasure, while ven older readers can learn truths from these allegories.

transcription: Lord Arthur Savile's crime' is the title of a bright little volume of stories by Oscar Wilde. The opnion once prevalent that Oscar Wilde was an impressionist crank was entirely erroneous. He can write pure, charming English and has a light, humorous fancy which is very attractive. He is certainly capable of more serious work if he choose to exert himself.
collection: Lord Arthur Savile’s Crime and Other Stories

transcription: The Happy Prince and other tales, by Oscar Wilde, illustrated by Walter Crane and Jacomb Hood, is a dainty and beautiful little volume of really meritorious literary character. Our old friend, Oscar Wilde, did not impress us formerly with his intellectual power or literary skill. He seemed to be the apostle of a limp and languid aestheticism, and the object of mild laughter. But we must acknowledge his real gift and grace in these very charming stories. The illustrations are, of course, excellent, and the typographical work is exquisitely done. Fairy tales ought always to be thus elegantly printed and illustrated. We congratulate Oscar, and thank him for his tales, and forgive him all his old trespasses agains the common sense of the age.
collection: The Happy Prince and Other Tales

transcription:
Dirty Linen
Los Angeles, April 10.—(To the Editor of The Times:)
Fair play is a word which should find an echo in every human heart; that it does not is obvious and likewise regrettable. A very scandalous lawsuit in London has just immersed in an unutterable mire an English author who, in his writings, has made it his pastime to taunt the Philistine. The Philistine is now tasting the sweets of revenge, and Oscar Wilde is nailed to the pillory of—what shall we call it?—righteous indignation or revolted public opinion.
His crime, if he be criminal, is one which in polite circles is labeled indecent; the most lenient critic would certainly call it unaesthetic. But here the philosophical doubt asserts itself—who is the more blamable of the two, Oscar Wilde, perhaps an unconscious erotomaniac, or the Marquis of Queensberry, that prize-ring tactician who drags his son into the nauseating cesspool wherein he would fain drown the author of The Picture of Dorian Gray?
Wilde may be a doomed man; but what is the future reserved, through the sottish interference of his father, to the youthful Lord Alfred Douglas? To put the question is to answer it. We have here once again the proof that inquisitorial zeal is worse than apathetic indifference. The mantle of charity covers a multitude of sins; and was it ever more necessary than in this sad case? The French are right: “Il faut laver son linge sale en famille.”
No one can deny that Oscar Wilde possesses in a high degree the marks of decadentism: its love of the artificial, its aversion to nature and all forms of activity and movement, its preference for diabolism, sadism, and unnatural depravity, its megalomaniacal contempt for man, and its exaggeration of the role of art.
His efforts may be classed under three headings: prose, poems, and plays. His poems are in the style of the Pre-Raphaelites, but are rather feeble imitations of Swinburne and Rossetti, and of dreary inanity. His prose works, on the contrary, are worthy of attention: Intentions, The Picture of Dorian Gray, Lord Arthur Savile’s Crime are very clever paradoxes; but that a paradox may become a reality Oscar Wilde seems to have once proven by taking on himself the part of Dorian Gray. The Happy Prince and others are very pretty tales.
His dramatic works comprise: Lady Windermere’s Fan, A Woman of No Importance, The Duchess of Padua, Salome, and An Ideal Husband. As a playwright, Oscar Wilde has been very successful; his pieces are the triumph of the epigram, and it must be conceded that he often uses this incisive weapon with great effect.
But alongside his literary efforts, even perhaps overtowering them, Wilde’s personal eccentricities have held the public in suspense, wondering what his next move would be.
He is fond of dressing in queer costumes which recall partly the fashions of the Middle Ages, partly the Rococo modes. He pretends to have given up the dress of the present time because it offends his sense of the beautiful; but this is only a pretext in which probably he himself does not believe, for when not posing I understand that he is rather inclined to be slovenly and to give the beautiful the “grand bounce,” so far as his vestments are concerned. What really determines his actions is the hysterical craving to be noticed, to occupy the attention of the world with himself, to get talked about. This craving must be more than amply satisfied just at present, in which moment he is for the first time in his life unwillingly the cynosure of all eyes.
It is a well-known fact that he once walked down Pall Mall in the afternoon dressed in doublet and breeches, with a picturesque biretta on his head and a sunflower in his hand, the quasi-heraldic symbol of the Aesthetes.
Oscar Wilde affects a superb disdain for nature, and says of her in his book Intentions: “Whatever actually occurs is spoiled for art. All bad poetry springs from genuine feeling. To be natural is to be obvious, and to be obvious is to be inartistic.”
He is a “cultivator of the ego,” and feels deliciously indignant at the fact that nature dares to be indifferent to the charms of his person. He writes: “Nature is so indifferent, so unappreciative. Whenever I am walking in the park here, I always feel that I am no more to her than the cattle that browse on the slope.”
With regard to himself and the human species, he says: “Ah, don’t say that you agree with me. When people agree with me I always feel that I must be wrong.”
These citations could be multiplied ad infinitum, but I shall close these lines with a poem by Oscar Wilde. It is rather appropriate:
TAEDIUM VITAE
“To stab my youth with desperate knives, to wear
This paltry age’s gaudy livery,
To let each base hand filch my treasury,
To mesh my soul within a woman’s hair,
And be mere Fortune’s lackeyed groom, I swear
I love it not. These things are less to me
Than the thin foam that frets upon the sea,
Less than the thistledown of summer air,
Which hath no seed; better to stand aloft
Far from these slanderous fools who mock my life,
Knowing me not; better the lowliest roof
Fit for the meanest hind to sojourn in,
Than even go back to that hoarse cave of strife
Where my white soul first kissed the mouth of sin.”

transcription: Mr. Oscar Wilde is about to follow the example of all who make a hit the field of fiction. He is going to republish some short stories contributed to the magazines in the days when he was a more notorious but less famous man than he now is. The temptation profitably to get rid of one's early work is, of course, very great, and it is seldom resisted, In the present case the author has done his best to Improve his youthful efforts by rewriting, recasting and revising where necessary. Four stories have been deemed worthy of republication: 'Lord Arthur Savile's Crime', 'The Canterville Ghost', 'The Sphinx Without a Secret' and 'A Model Millionaire'. The first-named story will give the volume its title. It will be published simultaneously in England and America.
collection: Lord Arthur Savile’s Crime and Other Stories

transcription:
They [Wilde's books] are unlikely to be removed from the Brooklyn Library.

The action of the City Library of St. Louis and the Free Library of Newark, N.J., in withdrawing from their shelves all books written by Oscar Wilde that may happen to be there is drawing some ridicule upon those institutions. The opinion most generally expressed is that the books are no worse now that Wilde’s personal wickedness is exposed than they were before; and if they contained nothing harmful when placed there, they have not been made harmful by the recent disclosures of the man’s character.

Willis A. Bardwell, librarian of the Brooklyn Library, smiled when a Standard Union reporter asked him what he thought of the action of the two libraries named.

“They are only advertising Wilde’s books,” he said. “If they don’t want people to read them, it would have been better for them to have let the matter alone. A man’s character does not necessarily affect his book, however; and if a work that is good in itself, or harmless, is to be condemned because a cloud comes over the reputation of its author, there would be no knowing where to begin or end the process of weeding out.

“The best way to create a demand for a bad book is to make it known that it is bad. If a bad book happens to find its way into a library collection, it is easy to withdraw it without making a fuss about it. “As to Wilde’s books, there are but a few of them in the Brooklyn Library, and there is no apparent reason why they should be withdrawn from circulation. Some of them are books for the young, and none of them are recent. We have bought none of his recent works, and, of course, would buy none now. But it isn’t at all likely that it will be necessary to destroy or hide those which we have.”

By examination of the catalogues, the reporter found that there are only six volumes of Wilde’s writings in the library. These are: 'Intentions', a collection of essays; 'Poems' (published in 1881); 'The Happy Prince and Other Tales'; 'A House of Pomegranates'; 'Lord Arthur Savile’s Crime, and Other Stories'; and 'The Young King and The Star-Child.''
collection: A House of Pomegranates

transcription: "Lord Arthur Savile's Crime, and Other Stories," by Oscar Wilde, is a vastly amusing little volume. It is cheap in price, it is artistically bound and printed, and the four tales it contains are all interesting. The two longer ones, "Lord Arthur Savile's Crime" and "The Canterville Ghost," are perhaps the best. The first of these is delightfully whimsical. Young Lord Arthur, engaged to the beautiful Sybil Merton—Mr. Oscar Wilde is evidently fond of the name Sybil—spends an evening with a duchess who has a fad for chiromancy. While there he is induced to have his hand examined by the eminent specialist Mr. Podgers. Podgers announces that the Lord Arthur is destined to commit a murder. This upsets the equanimity of the young man. Clearly he cannot marry Sybil—at least not till he has got the murder over. So he ruminates thereon, postpones the wedding, and decides to put away his estimable old aunt, Lady Clementina Beauchamp. This aged dame suffers from heartburn, so he prescribes one of his infallible pilules in which he has put a deadly dose of aconite. She promises to take it, but neglects to do so, and dies shortly afterwards a natural death. Thus foiled, Lord Arthur attempts to blow up the Dean of Chichester, another distant relative, with an alarum clock set with dynamite, but unluckily the thing won't work, and once more he has to put off the wedding. In the end he comes across the redoubtable Podgers himself strolling one dark night along the Embankment, so he catches him by the coat tails and flings him into the river. A few days afterwards, to his intense relief, he reads in the paper an announcement of the suicide of Podgers, and his conscience being thus clear he marries the beautiful Sybil, and all ends happily. "The Canterville Ghost" is perhaps not so amusing as Mr. Andrew Lang's "In Castle Perilous," of which it may remind the reader; but it is nevertheless very funny. It would be unfair to the author to say anything about the plot of this or the other two tales left; but readers may be assured that they will not be bored for a moment during the perusal of them. The little volume is not nearly so replete with paradoxical turns as the "Picture of Dorian Gray," but the good things are not wanting. Of the Lady Windermere the author says, "She was now forty years of age, childless, and with that inordinate passion for pleasure which is the secret of remaining young." "If a woman can't make her mistakes charming, she is only a female," says another. A young girl tries to persuade the ghost to emigrate to America, but the shade demurs on the ground that he thinks he would not like it. "I suppose because we have no ruins and no curiosities," says the maid, satirically. "No ruins! no curiosities!" answers the ghost. "You have your navy and your manners." Surely this is very excellent persiflage.
collection: Lord Arthur Savile’s Crime and Other Stories
